text: This video will demonstrate the laparoscopic approach for a closed-loop small bowel obstruction.
A seventy-nine-year-old female presented to the hospital with two days of abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting.
She had tenderness to palpation in the lower abdomen.
Her surgical history included a hysterectomy that had been performed many years in the past.
A CT scan was performed which revealed a distended loop of intestine concerning for a closed-loop obstruction.
Given these findings, the patient was brought to the operating room for a laparoscopic exploration.
The surgeon observes ischemic bowel in the pelvis, characterized by necrotic and hemorrhagic mesentery.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to transect the adhesion from the patient's prior hysterectomy, unraveling it to relieve the closed-loop obstruction in the intestine.
The surgeon transects the remaining end of the adhesion band, allowing the bowel to be delivered from the pelvis.
The surgeon visualizes the extent of the bowel injury after resolving the obstruction, revealing areas of ischemic and potentially necrotic tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates one of the two transition points in the small bowel, highlighting the change in bowel appearance at this level.
The surgeon plans a resection due to significant bowel ischemia and necrotic mesentery, as observed in the surgical field.
A 12mm laparoscopic port is placed to facilitate the use of a laparoscopic stapler during the small bowel resection procedure.
The surgeon begins the mesenteric transection, creating a window at the level of healthy intestine, visually distinguishing the viable tissue from the necrotic areas.
The surgeon places a laparoscopic stapler across the intestine after completing the transection of the mesentery at the specified level, preparing for resection.
The powered stapler is visible within the abdomen, allowing the surgeon to make smooth and precise adjustments during the small bowel resection.
The surgeon continues the mesenteric dissection after transecting the bowel at the specified level, using laparoscopic instruments to separate the mesentery from the bowel.
The surgeon transects the mesentery below the level of necrotic tissue using a laparoscopic stapler, ensuring a proper seal to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the mesentery until reaching an area of healthy intestine, ensuring the transection occurs at a viable section of the bowel.
The surgeon introduces the laparoscopic stapler into the abdomen and transects the bowel, completing the separation of the intestinal segment.
The surgeon uses an energy device to ligate the final mesenteric attachments, completing the separation of the bowel from its mesenteric connections.
The surgeon removes the resected bowel specimen from the operative field, clearing the area for further surgical steps.
An assistant helps create a window on the anti-mesenteric border of the small intestine, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon removes a small piece of bowel from the patient's abdomen under direct visualization, ensuring the specimen is completely extracted from the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a window at the anti-mesenteric border of the small intestine at the other staple line, similar to the previous step.
The surgeon aligns the bowel in an anti-peristaltic fashion, preparing the tissue for the formation of the intracorporeal anastomosis.
The use of an assistant is integral to this portion of the procedure.
The surgeon uses two graspers to carefully position the stapler through the intestine, ensuring the bowel remains securely aligned with the stapler.
The surgeon forms the common channel by aligning the bowel in the correct position, preparing for the intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon reintroduces the stapler into the intestine to lengthen the common channel, ensuring precise alignment of the bowel for the intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon measures the final length of the common channel, which is approximately ten centimeters.
The surgeon removes the stapler, revealing the anastomosis site, which is inspected and shows no signs of bleeding.
The surgeon uses graspers to hold the bowel in place while closing the common enterotomy with a stapler, ensuring the bowel remains aligned during the stapling process.
The importance of forming an initially long common channel is seen here.
After closing the stapler, the surgeon examines both sides of the bowel to confirm that the closure is complete and secure.
The surgeon uses the stapler to close approximately seventy-five percent of the common enterotomy, visibly reducing the opening in the bowel.
The surgeon reintroduces the stapler to complete the anastomosis, ensuring the bowel segments are securely joined with no visible bleeding.
The surgeon utilizes the initially long common channel to perform the anastomosis, ensuring the integrity of the connection between the bowel segments.
The completed small bowel anastomosis is visibly patent, showing a clear and unobstructed channel between the connected bowel segments.
The surgeon grasps a small portion of the bowel from the closure of the anastomosis along with the main specimen, indicating the completion of the resection.
The surgeon enlarges the twelve millimeter port site and places a small wound protector to maintain access and protect the incision during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a Babcock clamp through the wound protector to extract the bowel specimens, avoiding the need for a larger separate extraction site.
The surgeon irrigates and suctions the patient's pelvis, ensuring thorough cleaning after the removal of the bowel specimens.
The surgeon examines the bowel, confirming the anastomosis is in excellent position with no visible twisting of the mesentery.
The surgeon visually inspects the widely patent anastomosis, confirming its proper alignment and integrity without any twisting of the mesentery.
The surgeon uses a twelve millimeter clip applier to grasp and close the two leaves of the small mesenteric defect, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon places clips along the mesenteric defect until it is completely closed, ensuring secure closure of the defect.
The surgeon visually inspects the completed closure of the mesenteric defect, ensuring the defect is fully closed with clips.
The surgeon removes the wound protector from the patient's abdomen through the twelve millimeter port site, completing the procedure.
The surgeon observes the large fascial defect, noting its potential as a future site for herniation.
The surgeon uses an endoclosed device with an absorbable suture to close the fascial defect in a figure of eight pattern, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.
The video displays the patient's postoperative abdomen, showing the external appearance following the surgical procedure.
The surgeon highlights the left upper quadrant incision, marked by a red circle, which serves as the extraction site for the small bowel resection.
The surgical specimen is displayed at the bottom of the screen, providing a clear view of the resected tissue.
This patient had an uneventful postoperative course.
On postoperative day one, she was passing flatus and tolerating liquids.
Her diet was advanced and she was discharged to her home on post-operative day three.
The patient returned to the office two weeks later with no complaints.
This video demonstrated a minimally invasive technique in the emergent setting.
With proper experience, this is safe and leads to improved patient outcomes.
